title: Beam Search on Meaning: How Minds and Machines Resolve Ambiguity
subtitle: From compiler parsers to garden path sentences to transformer attention — the same algorithm for navigating interpretation space

props.title: The Problem: Too Many Meanings, Not Enough Time

children.children: Every time you encounter an ambiguous word, your brain faces a combinatorial explosion. 'Bank' has at least 8 meanings. 'Set' has over 400. In a sentence with three ambiguous words, the number of possible interpretations MULTIPLIES — 8 × 12 × 5 = 480 candidate readings.

You can't evaluate all of them. You don't. Instead, you use a strategy that compilers, search engines, and neural networks have all independently converged on: explore the most promising paths, keep backup candidates on a shelf, and backtrack only when forced.
This strategy has a name: beam search. And it appears to be a universal algorithm for navigating interpretation spaces.
Exhaustive Search (What You Can't Do)
Imagine exploring every possible interpretation simultaneously:

"The bank was steep"

                    bank
                   / | \
                  /  |  \
        financial  river  pool table
          / \      / \      / \
        ...  ... ...  ... ...  ...

→ Exponential explosion. Impossible.
Greedy Search (What's Dangerous)
Pick the single most likely interpretation at each step. Fast but fragile — one wrong commitment and you're stuck:

"The bank was steep and muddy near the water"

Greedy: bank → financial (most frequent meaning)
        ... steep? That's weird for a building.
        ... muddy? Buildings aren't muddy.
        ... near the water? OH. River bank.

→ Committed too early. Expensive reanalysis needed.
Beam Search (The Sweet Spot)
Keep the top-k candidates alive. Actively process the best one. Shelve the rest. Prune when capacity runs out.

"The bank was steep"

Beam width = 3:

  Step 1: "bank"
    → [financial: 0.45, river: 0.35, pool: 0.10, ...]
    → Keep top 3: financial(0.45), river(0.35), pool(0.10)

  Step 2: "was steep"
    → financial + steep → unlikely (0.45 × 0.1 = 0.045)
    → river + steep → very likely (0.35 × 0.9 = 0.315)
    → pool + steep → unlikely (0.10 × 0.05 = 0.005)

  Rerank: river(0.315), financial(0.045), pool(0.005)
          ^^^^^ river is now dominant!

  Step 3: "and muddy"
    → river + muddy → confirmed (0.315 × 0.95 = 0.299)
    → financial + muddy → dead (0.045 × 0.01 = 0.0005)

  Result: RIVER BANK. Financial shelved then expired.
The 'beam width' is how many candidates you keep alive. Width 1 = greedy (dangerous). Width ∞ = exhaustive (impossible). Width 3-10 = practical (what brains and machines actually do).
Compiler designers faced this exact problem in the 1960s. When parsing source code, ambiguities arise constantly:

int x;        // Is 'x' a variable declaration...
int x = 5;    // ...or a variable definition with initialization?

f(x);         // Is this a function call...
f (x);        // ...or a variable declaration with redundant parens?
              // (In some languages, both are valid!)
LR Parsing: Shift and Reduce
LR parsers read tokens left-to-right and maintain a stack of partial interpretations. At each token, they either:

items.definition: Push the token onto the stack. 'I need more information before deciding.' This is SHELVING.
Pop items off the stack and combine them. 'I know what this is now.' This is COMMITTING.

children.children: When the grammar is ambiguous (two valid reductions possible), the parser has a 'shift-reduce conflict' — the mechanical equivalent of the brain encountering 'bank.'
GLR Parsers: Fork the Parse
Generalized LR parsers (Tomita, 1985) solve this by doing exactly what beam search does: fork the stack into parallel alternatives and process both simultaneously.

Token stream: type  name  (  args  )

Stack 1: [function_declaration name (args) ]  ← function declaration
Stack 2: [type name (expression) ]             ← variable with cast

Both stacks process in parallel until one becomes
invalid (missing semicolon, type mismatch, etc.)
→ Invalid stack is pruned
→ Surviving stack is the correct parse

GLR parsers are beam search on syntax trees. The beam width is dynamic — forks happen at ambiguities and die at contradictions. The same algorithm that (we argue) runs in your head when you read 'The bank was steep.'
The C++ Parsing Nightmare
C++ is famously ambiguous to parse. The statement:
T(a);
Could be:
A function call: call function T with argument a
A variable declaration: declare variable a of type T (with redundant parentheses)
A cast: cast a to type T

The compiler must keep all three interpretations alive until enough context resolves the ambiguity. Sometimes it takes MANY more tokens — this is the compiler's 'garden path.' C++ compilers essentially run beam search on the interpretation space of your code.

props.title: Garden Path Sentences: When the Brain's Beam Search Fails

children.children: Psycholinguists have been studying how the brain handles ambiguity for decades. Garden path sentences are the key experimental tool — sentences designed to trick your parser into committing to the wrong interpretation.
Classic Examples
Read each sentence and notice where you stumble:
"The horse raced past the barn fell." — You commit to 'raced' as the main verb. Then 'fell' arrives and you have to reparse: the horse [that was] raced past the barn... fell.
"The old man the boats." — 'Old' seems like an adjective. But 'man' is the verb here: the old [people] man the boats.

"Time flies like an arrow; fruit flies like a banana." — In the second clause, 'flies' is a noun and 'like' is a verb. Your parser doesn't catch this because it committed to the pattern from the first clause.
"The complex houses married and single soldiers and their families." — 'Complex' is a noun (housing complex), not an adjective. 'Houses' is a verb, not a noun.
What's Happening in Beam Search Terms

"The horse raced past the barn fell."

  "The horse"    → [subject: horse] (only one reading, no ambiguity)
  "raced"        → FORK:
                    Beam 1: horse raced (main verb, active) → score: 0.85
                    Beam 2: horse [that was] raced (reduced relative) → score: 0.15
  "past the barn" → Both beams compatible, scores unchanged
  "fell"         → CRASH:
                    Beam 1: horse raced past the barn... fell? TWO MAIN VERBS? → score: 0.0
                    Beam 2: horse [that was] raced past the barn fell → score: 0.15

  Beam 1 dies. Beam 2 survives.
  But Beam 2 was low-probability → REANALYSIS (expensive, the 'huh?' moment)

The garden path effect occurs when the correct interpretation was kept in the beam but with very low probability. Reanalysis succeeds (you eventually parse the sentence) but it's costly — measurable in eye-tracking as regressions, in reading time as delays, and subjectively as the 'huh?' feeling.
When Reanalysis Fails: Beam Too Narrow

Sometimes the correct interpretation was PRUNED — it fell off the beam entirely. When this happens, people report the sentence as ungrammatical even though it's perfectly valid. The shelved candidate expired before the disambiguating evidence arrived.

This is the human equivalent of a beam width that's too narrow. Your parser kept only the top 2-3 interpretations, the correct one was ranked 4th, and it got pruned. No amount of rereading helps immediately — you need someone to explain the parse, at which point you go 'OH!' and can see it.

Garden path failures are evidence that the brain does NOT exhaustively evaluate all interpretations. It runs a limited-width beam search and sometimes the beam is too narrow. This is the cost of being fast — you occasionally commit to the wrong parse and pay the reanalysis penalty.

props.title: Transformers: Beam Search in the Residual Stream

children.children: Now the connection to neural networks. When a transformer processes an ambiguous word, the residual stream carries multiple interpretations simultaneously — literally a beam of candidates encoded as superposed features.
The Residual Stream as Parallel Parse Stacks

Input: "He sat on the bank and watched the current"

Layer 2 residual stream for "bank":
  SAE features active:
    financial_institution: 0.45  ← Beam candidate 1
    river_bank: 0.35             ← Beam candidate 2
    data_bank: 0.05              ← Beam candidate 3 (barely surviving)
    pool_bank: 0.02              ← Below threshold (pruned by ReLU)

Layer 6 (after "sat on the"):
    river_bank: 0.72             ← Boosted by physical context
    financial_institution: 0.15  ← Suppressed but SHELVED
    data_bank: 0.01              ← Fading

Layer 10 (after "watched the current"):
    river_bank: 0.55             ← Still dominant but...
    financial_institution: 0.25  ← "current" boosts this too!
    → Ambiguity PARTIALLY UNRESOLVED
    → Both survive until more context arrives

The ReLU in each layer acts as the beam pruner. Features below threshold get zeroed — pruned from the beam. Features above threshold survive to the next layer. Attention heads are the scoring mechanism that reranks candidates at each step.
Attention as Contextual Reranking
What attention heads actually do in this framework:
Look back at context tokens ('sat', 'on', 'watched', 'current')
Compute relevance (dot product — it's always dot products)
Pull contextual evidence into the residual stream
This evidence boosts compatible interpretations and suppresses incompatible ones
The beam is reranked. Some candidates rise, others fall.

Each transformer layer is one step of beam search: score the current candidates against new evidence (attention), rerank (MLP), prune (ReLU). 12 layers = 12 rounds of evidence accumulation and pruning. The residual stream IS the beam.
We can formalize the beam search process as four operators — distinct computational events that occur during ambiguity resolution.

rows: Promote one interpretation to dominant. Suppress (don't eliminate) others.
Sharp drop in losing features between layers
The moment you 'decide' what a word means
Continue processing under current interpretation. No reranking.
Active interpretation hits contradiction. Swap to shelved candidate.
Weak features overwritten by new information
Silent. You never notice what you can no longer consider.

children.children: A Testable Model
This operator taxonomy makes specific, testable predictions about transformer internals:

COMMIT should be visible as a sharp drop in the activation of the 'losing' SAE feature between two adjacent layers — not a gradual fade. If 'river' drops from 0.35 to 0.05 between layer 5 and layer 6, that's a phase transition, not decay.
MAINTAIN should show stable feature activations across multiple layers — the 'committed' feature stays roughly constant while no contradictory evidence arrives.

REANALYZE should show a sudden REVERSAL — a previously suppressed feature spikes back up at a specific layer when contradictory context arrives. This should correlate with the garden path effect in reading time data.

EXPIRE should show a monotonic, gradual decay of shelved features — and critically, once a feature drops below some threshold, REANALYZE becomes impossible (the model can no longer recover that interpretation).
The EXPIRE threshold should correlate with the human 'distance effect' — reanalysis difficulty increasing with the number of tokens between ambiguity and disambiguation.

All five predictions are testable TODAY using existing SAE tools (SAE Lens, Neuronpedia) on open-source models (GPT-2, Pythia). Run ambiguous sentences through the model, extract SAE features at every layer, and look for these signatures.
Experimental Design Sketch

# Pseudocode for testing the disambiguation operators

import sae_lens

# Prepare sentences with controlled ambiguity
sentences = {
    'early_resolve': 'The river bank was steep and muddy',
    'late_resolve':  'The bank was visible from far away near the river',
    'never_resolve': 'He sat on the bank and watched the current',
    'garden_path':   'The horse raced past the barn fell',
}

for name, sentence in sentences.items():
    # Get SAE features at every layer
    features_by_layer = extract_sae_features(model, sae, sentence)
    
    # Track specific features across layers
    financial = [features_by_layer[L]['financial_institution'] for L in range(12)]
    river     = [features_by_layer[L]['river_bank'] for L in range(12)]
    
    # Look for:
    # - COMMIT: sharp divergence point between financial and river
    # - MAINTAIN: plateau after commitment
    # - REANALYZE: reversal (if it occurs)
    # - EXPIRE: gradual decay of losing candidate
    
    plot_feature_trajectories(name, financial, river)
Architectural Proposal: Stack-Based Backtracking

Beyond testing the current architecture, we can propose a better one. Instead of relying on emergent disambiguation from attention (one primitive doing everything), add explicit disambiguation machinery:

STACK-BASED DISAMBIGUATION ARCHITECTURE

INTERPRETATION STACK        RESIDUAL STREAM
                              
[financial, L4] ←PUSH        ──■──────────────→
                               ↑ bookmark
                               (save residual snapshot)

Processing continues with committed interpretation...

[financial, L4]              ──■──●──●──●──●──→
                                  layers 5-9

Layer 10: "near the river" → CONTRADICTION DETECTED
  coherence = dot(river_evidence, financial_dir) < threshold

POP stack → financial
REWIND to bookmark (layer 4 state)
COMMIT to river interpretation

[river, L4]                  ──■──→ re-process from here
                               ↑ rewind to bookmark
Three rewind mechanisms, from simple to elegant:

rows: Save full KV-cache at bookmark, restore and re-run layers

children.children: This is cheaper than forking the stream into parallel paths (the GLR approach). Instead of running two interpreters, you run one and save checkpoints. It's closer to how humans seem to work: commit fast, backtrack only when forced. The stack depth = your beam width.

props.title: The Convergence: Three Fields, One Algorithm

children.children: Compilers, brains, and neural networks all converge on the same solution to the same problem:

This convergence isn't coincidence. It's a consequence of the shared constraint: limited resources (stack space / neural capacity / embedding dimensions) facing combinatorial ambiguity. Beam search is the optimal strategy under bounded computation, regardless of substrate.
This disambiguation model plugs directly into the Schankian operator framework:
COMMIT, MAINTAIN, REANALYZE, and EXPIRE are operators in their own right — transformations on the interpretation state, with preconditions and postconditions
Polysemy resolution ('shot him a glance' vs 'shot him dead') is beam search over the operator tree — the hierarchical operator tree (Paper 2c) IS the search space
The shelved interpretations are exactly the implicit state that the enrichment framework (Paper 2a) would annotate: 'at this point, RIVER_BANK is shelved at probability 0.15'
Garden path recovery is operator REANALYZE — a phase transition in interpretation space, with measurable computational cost

The deeper point: understanding language isn't just about applying operators to entities. It's about navigating a space of POSSIBLE operator applications, committing to the most likely, and maintaining the ability to backtrack. The beam search IS the comprehension process.
Beam search keeps the top-k interpretations alive, processes the best one, shelves the rest, and prunes when capacity runs out.
Compilers (GLR parsers) fork the parse stack at ambiguities and run parallel parses — beam search on syntax.
Human brains commit fast to the best interpretation but keep alternatives shelved. Garden path sentences reveal the reanalysis cost when the commitment was wrong.
Transformers encode the beam as superposed features in the residual stream. Attention reranks, ReLU prunes, and layers accumulate evidence.
Four disambiguation operators (COMMIT, MAINTAIN, REANALYZE, EXPIRE) formalize the process with testable predictions about feature trajectories across layers.
The convergence across compilers, brains, and neural networks isn't coincidence — it's the optimal strategy for bounded computation facing combinatorial ambiguity.

This tutorial is part of the Schankian Operators research program. The disambiguation operators connect to: the hierarchical operator tree (Paper 2c) as the search space, implicit-explicit enrichment (Paper 2a) for annotating shelved interpretations, and SAE mining (Brainstorm #25) for finding the features that participate in beam search.
